name: 12 visual design principles
description: Size/scale, contrast, alignment, emphasis, typography, repetition, whitespace, proximity, movement, proportion, perspective, reading patterns — applied as a practical UI review.

metadata.intent: Use 12 visual design principles to improve hierarchy, readability, and scan-ability in UI

must_use: A consistent scale system for typography and spacing
must_not: Overemphasize everything (if everything is loud, nothing is)
conventions: Prefer clear reading patterns (F/Z) for information-dense layouts.

steps.description: Size & scale: define a fixed type scale (H1..H6) and spacing scale; use meaningful jumps (3x+) for true hierarchy.
Color & contrast: reserve high contrast for primary actions; ensure accessible contrast for text; use tokens/variables for consistency.
Alignment: grid discipline reduces confusion; misalignment must be intentional and rare.
Emphasis: choose one focal point per screen/section; use scale, position, weight, or color — not all at once.
Typography: balance weights and line lengths; keep body readable; use bolding sparingly to preserve meaning.
Repetition: reuse components, spacing, icon style, and interaction patterns to reduce learning cost and reinforce brand.
Whitespace: avoid clutter; create breathing room so users can scan; dense ≠ cluttered if spacing is deliberate.
Proximity: group related controls; separate destructive actions; use spacing to communicate relationships.
Movement: guide the eye (lines, progressive disclosure, animation) toward the next action; avoid distracting motion.
Proportion: ensure elements feel related; CTAs, images, and text blocks should support the same story and goal.
Perspective/depth: use subtle depth (shadows, layers) to clarify affordances; avoid fake depth that hurts accessibility.
Reading patterns: design for F/Z scanning; headings should be meaning-bearing; avoid 'layer cake' pages where only headers are readable.
